content:
Design Patterns

Artificial Intelligence

Software Development

Vibe Coding

Programming

9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image Generated by AII once shipped a payment module with a switch statement that I knew, even while typing it, should have been the Strategy pattern. The refactor would have taken two more days. The deadline would not move. The switch statement won.
That is not a confession of bad engineering. That is how most software actually gets built. In all my years of writing code around delivery dates, design patterns were the thing we praised in code reviews and quietly cut when the sprint got tight.
AI coding tools have changed that math completely, and I don’t think enough people have noticed. When the AI does the typing, the pattern no longer costs you two days. It costs you one sentence in a prompt. So let’s talk about why design patterns should move from “nice to have” to first priority — and how to actually make your AI apply them.
What Is a Design Pattern, Really?
A design pattern is a named, reusable solution to a recurring software design problem — a proven structure you adapt to your code, not a library you import. Patterns like Strategy, Adapter, and Observer describe how the parts of a system should relate to each other so the code stays easy to change later.
The Gang of Four catalogued 23 of them back in 1994. Languages have since absorbed a few (nobody hand-writes an Iterator anymore), and architectural patterns like MVC and Repository joined the family. But the purpose never changed: patterns exist so that the next person who touches the code can understand and extend it without breaking something.
Notice what patterns were never about — making code run faster or getting features out the door sooner. Which is exactly why they kept losing.

Why Patterns Always Lost to the Deadline
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image Generated by AIDesign patterns were never skipped because they stopped working; they were skipped because they cost extra human hours upfront. The pattern paid off months later, in maintenance, while the deadline was due Friday. Any benefit you can only collect next quarter loses to a cost you must pay this week.
I have watched this play out on team after team. Everyone agrees the abstraction is right. Everyone also agrees the demo is Thursday. So the “temporary” hard-coded version goes in, a TODO comment goes on top, and three years later that TODO is load-bearing.
Though here’s the thing — we were not wrong to make that trade. Hand-writing a Strategy pattern genuinely takes longer than a switch statement. Extracting an interface, wiring dependency injection, setting up the factory — that is real effort when a human types every line. The economics honestly favored the shortcut.
Then the economics changed.

AI Flipped the Cost Equation
When an AI assistant writes the code, the marginal cost of doing it properly drops to almost nothing. The AI types the interface, the three implementations, and the wiring in the same minute it would have typed the switch statement. This means the old excuse — “the clean version takes too long” — is simply gone.
But there’s a second half to this, and it’s the part that worries me more. AI doesn’t just make good structure cheap. It makes bad structure cheap too, and at a volume no human team could ever produce by hand.
Left unguided, an AI assistant will happily generate a 400-line function that works. It compiles, the tests pass, the feature demos fine. Six months later nobody — including the AI — can safely modify it. Researchers have started calling this pile-up comprehension debt: code that runs in production while the understanding of it exists nowhere. When code was expensive to produce, bad structure accumulated slowly. Now it accumulates at generation speed.
So the situation is asymmetric. The cost of applying patterns collapsed, and the cost of not applying them exploded. In my mind, that settles the priority question.

Patterns Are Now a Prompt Language
Here’s the part I find genuinely elegant. Design patterns were invented as a shared vocabulary between humans — say “Observer” and a colleague instantly pictures the whole structure. It turns out that vocabulary works on AI models too, and it works brilliantly.
A pattern name acts as a compressed instruction to an AI coding assistant. The single word “Strategy” in a prompt replaces a full paragraph describing interfaces, implementations, and injection points. The model has seen thousands of well-formed examples of every classic pattern, so naming one steers it toward proven structure instead of whatever shape falls out of token prediction.
In practice, the difference is dramatic.
“Add support for PayPal payments” gets you PayPal logic stuffed wherever the credit card logic lives.
“Refactor payment handling to the Strategy pattern, then add PayPal as a new strategy” gets you an extension point — and every payment method you add after that is a new file, not a bigger diff.
You spent twenty years building this vocabulary to talk to other developers. Now use it to talk to the machine.

How to Make AI Apply Design Patterns When Vibe Coding
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image Generated by AIVibe coding — describing what you want conversationally and letting the AI build it — is where structure dies fastest, because nobody is steering. These are the habits that fixed it for me.
1. Ask for a design before any code
End your feature request with: “Before writing code, propose a design and name the patterns you’ll use.” This one sentence turns the AI from a typist into a design partner, and you get to veto a bad structure before it exists.
2. Name the pattern when you know it
Don’t describe the shape you want — name it. “Use the Adapter pattern to wrap this API” beats three sentences of structural description, and the output is far more predictable.
3. Make the AI justify its structure
When it proposes something, ask “why this pattern and not X?” If the justification is weak, you’ve caught a bad design at the cheapest possible moment. I do this even when I agree, because sometimes the answer surprises me.
4. Request the refactor you’d never budget for
See a switch statement growing its fourth case? Say “refactor this to Strategy” and review the diff. The two-day refactor from my payment-module story is now a five-minute conversation. That still feels a little unreal to me.
5. Make the agent iterate on its own design
Working code is a draft, not a destination. Once the feature runs, send one more prompt: “Review this implementation — would a design pattern make it simpler or easier to extend? Propose the refactor.” The agent will often spot the emerging Strategy or Adapter it missed on the first pass, and the second iteration costs minutes.
This is really the old red-green-refactor loop coming back. We always skipped the refactor step because it was the expensive one. Now it’s the cheapest — so put it back in the loop and let the agent run it.
6. Review structure first, lines second
When reviewing AI output, check the shape before the syntax. Wrong variable name costs a minute. Wrong structure compounds forever.

Baking Patterns into Spec-Driven Development
Prompting habits are good, but they depend on you remembering. Spec-driven development — where the AI works from a written specification instead of a chat message — lets you make patterns the default rather than a request.
Two places to encode them. First, your project instruction file (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, or your tool’s equivalent) should state your architectural rules once so every session inherits them: which patterns the project uses, where new integrations go, what needs an interface. I covered how I structure these files in my post on AI coding ground rules, and the architecture section has quietly become the most valuable part of mine.
Second, make the spec itself carry the design. Every spec should define component boundaries and communication contracts — which component owns which responsibility, and how they talk to each other: the interfaces, the events, the data shapes crossing each boundary.
This is the raw material for pattern thinking, because most patterns simply are boundary structure. Adapter, Facade, Strategy, Observer — each one describes how two parts of a system should communicate. Hand the AI explicit boundaries and contracts, and it can reason its way to the right pattern instead of guessing at one.
Though there’s a gap here I nearly missed. Boundaries and contracts steer structural and behavioral patterns nicely, because both describe how parts talk. They say nothing about how those parts come to exist — the territory of creational patterns. And this is the one area where I would not trust the AI’s defaults, because object creation is cross-cutting: leave it unguided and new calls (or worse, Singletons — AI loves a Singleton) quietly spread through every file the agent touches.
So give the spec a third input: lifecycle and ownership rules. State the lifetimes (what lives for the whole application, what’s per-request, what’s throwaway), who is allowed to construct what, and where the wiring happens — your composition root or DI container.
With those constraints written down, the selection itself is safe to delegate. Whether the agent reaches for a Factory Method, a Builder, or plain constructor injection is a choice it can make — and justify — inside rules you set. The constraints are yours; the mechanism is negotiable.
Then add a mandatory Design Approach section on top — the patterns to apply, the extension points to preserve. A spec that only lists behavior (“the system shall export reports as PDF”) licenses the AI to bolt PDF logic anywhere. One that adds “export formats follow the existing Strategy pattern; register a new PdfExporter” produces code that belongs to the architecture instead of leaning against it.
The principle running through all of this is the same: never leave structure to chance. Behavior is what the software does; structure is whether you can change what it does. Specs have always been good at the first and silent on the second — and with AI, silence is a decision.

One Honest Caveat: Don’t Let the AI Over-Pattern
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image Generated by AII need to flag the opposite failure, because AI commits it eagerly. Ask for “proper design patterns” with no context and you may receive an AbstractFactoryProviderManager for a feature with one implementation. AI models can be pattern-happy the way junior developers fresh off the GoF book are pattern-happy.
The fix is the same discipline as before, pointed the other way. Patterns earn their place by solving a problem you actually have — a second implementation, a genuine need to decouple. When the AI proposes an abstraction, ask what concrete problem it solves. “Flexibility we might need later” is not an answer; that’s speculation with extra files.
So the goal was never “more patterns.” It’s deliberate structure — chosen by you, produced by the AI.

Patterns First Is Finally Affordable
For most of my career, design patterns were the right answer we couldn’t afford. The deadline always outranked the abstraction, and honestly, the trade often made sense. AI has ended that era: good structure now costs a sentence, while unstructured code piles up faster than anyone can comprehend it.
The developers who thrive with AI won’t be the fastest prompters. They’ll be the ones who know which structure to ask for — and never leave it to chance.
Next time you’re about to accept working-but-shapeless AI code, try one sentence first: “Propose a design and name the patterns.” You’ll rarely go back.
